Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm 28, so I've got a lot going on outside of school, and I found myself squeezing in study time whenever I could rather than having a set schedule. I learn best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often spend time working through problems or activities rather than just reading. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd binge-watch lecture videos late at night when my anxiety was keeping me up anyway.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were looming. I wish I could say I was consistent, but between work and personal stuff, regular study times just weren't realistic for me. I'd often find myself cramming right before something was due, which I know isn't ideal but it's how I've always functioned. The anxiety would spike and suddenly I'd be productive.
Q3: Usually it was either seeing a notification about something due soon or just feeling guilty that I hadn't logged in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in just to scroll through and see what I'd missed, even if I wasn't planning to do much. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because there was something almost addictive about checking my progress, even when I was feeling sad and unmotivated about everything else.
Q4: The gamified elements kind of tricked me into making it routine for a while. I'd log in to check badges or see where I stood, and that became almost habitual for a few weeks. But then life would get in the way and the routine would fall apart again. It never really stuck permanently, but there were stretches where checking the LMS felt like checking social media.
Q5: The video content was genuinely helpful because I could watch it multiple times and pause to work through things myself. I watched over 200 minutes of videos because that format just clicks for me better than reading dense text. The discussion boards were also useful when people shared practical examples or different ways of understanding concepts. I need to see things applied, not just theorized about.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and any activities that felt too passive. If something required me to just sit and read pages of text, I'd skim it at best or skip it entirely. I also delayed a lot of the assignments, which shows in my terrible completion rate. It wasn't that I didn't think they were important, I just couldn't bring myself to start them when I was feeling low.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate practical application or when it gave me some kind of feedback or reward. The gamified tasks were good at this because I could see progress happening. I also valued anything that let me engage actively rather than passively absorbing information. If I couldn't touch it, manipulate it, or apply it somehow, my brain would just check out.
Q8: Required activities felt like weights on my chest, especially with my anxiety. I'd stress about them constantly but still avoid doing them. Optional activities were weirdly easier to engage with because there was no pressure, no consequences if I didn't do them perfectly. I'd sometimes do optional discussion posts but skip required assignments, which makes no sense logically but that's how my brain works.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and worked through them offline, especially when I was commuting or just wanted to get away from screens. I'd print things out and annotate them by hand, or work through problems on paper. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources to understand concepts in different ways. A lot of my actual learning happened completely outside the LMS.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be sitting with my notes spread out, working through problems or re-watching downloaded videos, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also have study sessions with a friend where we'd talk through concepts for hours. My exam score was decent, which I think reflects learning that happened off-platform. The LMS only saw a fraction of my effort.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a video or PDF once and then use it for weeks without logging back in. My laptop had a whole folder of course materials that I'd return to repeatedly. It was easier to study that way because I didn't need internet access and I could organize things how I wanted. One download could represent hours of subsequent offline study.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt impossible. When my sadness was at its worst, even logging in felt like too much effort. I'd go through phases where everything felt pointless and I couldn't see why any of this mattered. My anxiety would also paralyze me sometimes, making me avoid the LMS entirely because seeing what I'd missed would make me feel worse.
Q13: My competing responsibilities definitely impacted my LMS use significantly. Being 28 means I have adult obligations that younger students might not have. Fatigue was a huge factor too, especially combined with my anxiety keeping me up at night. When I was overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing to get dropped because it felt like the most optional part of my life.
Q14: Oh, definitely. I'd sometimes log in and just stare at the screen, clicking through things without really processing anything. My body was there but my mind was somewhere else, usually spiraling about something. I'd watch videos but realize at the end I hadn't absorbed a single thing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions to ease my guilt rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible, like there was a wall between me and the task. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. It's a terrible cycle because the avoidance made my anxiety worse, which made me avoid more. My 13% assignment completion rate tells that story clearly.
Q16: Deadlines created panic spikes that actually got me moving. Without a deadline, I could drift indefinitely, but when something was due soon, the urgency would override my avoidance patterns. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before due dates. It wasn't healthy, but deadlines were basically the only external force strong enough to push through my mental barriers.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback could boost my mood temporarily and make me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even constructive criticism, would sometimes send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. My resilience usually brought me back eventually, but that initial sting could derail my engagement significantly.
Q18: The course structure was honestly pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work at my own pace when I was struggling. Bad because without rigid structure, I'd let things slide. The gamified elements added some structure that helped, giving me smaller goals to work toward. But overall, I needed more external scaffolding than was provided.
Q19: The gamification genuinely made engagement easier because it broke things into smaller, achievable chunks and gave me that dopamine hit of progress. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content and the lack of clear prioritization. When everything seems equally important, my anxious brain gets overwhelmed and shuts down. Clearer pathways would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I can completely relate to that pattern. Sometimes you're in the LMS trying to figure things out, watching videos, reading discussions, genuinely attempting to engage, but you can't bring yourself to actually submit work. The submission part requires a different kind of energy, a commitment that feels terrifying when you're anxious about being judged. Activity doesn't equal output when your mental health is struggling.
Q21: That's basically me. My attendance and assignment completion were terrible, but I still got a Merit grade because I was learning in ways the system couldn't see. I studied offline, I prepared for exams in my own way, I understood the material even when I couldn't demonstrate it through regular submissions. The LMS data makes me look like a disaster, but my exam score tells a different story.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people are online and what they click on, which is useful for identifying who's completely absent. But they miss so much, like offline study, emotional state, quality of engagement versus just presence, and learning that happens through conversations or external resources. They measure activity, not learning. They see behavior, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. For me, engagement looks like wrestling with concepts until they make sense, applying ideas to real situations, and feeling connected to the material. It's not about how many times I logged in or how many boxes I checked. It's about whether the learning actually stuck.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety attacks before assignments, my late-night offline study sessions, the conversations with friends that helped concepts click, or the genuine interest I had in the material despite my terrible participation metrics. It can't show that I was sad and struggling but still trying in my own way. It sees a disengaged student, but I was engaged, just not in ways that left digital footprints.
